## 2.2. Nenitzescu reaction

The classical Nenitzescu synthesis of 5-hydroxyindoles comprises the condensation of p-benzoquinones with aminocrotonates (Scheme 22). 49

This method still has not lost its significance, as indicated by the continuously appearing publications reporting its modification. In 2010, it was demonstrated that this method is applicable for the industrial production of 5-hydroxy-2-methyl-1H-indole-3-carboxylic acid (in 65 kg quantity) and 5-hydroxy-2-methyl-1H-indole (in 20 kg quantity). 50 The replacement of p-benzoquinone by quinoneimines 32, which are readily obtained by the oxidation of the appropriate p-alkoxyanilines, and the subsequent condensation with hexafluoroacetylacetone (in the Nenitzescu synthesis, aminocrotonates are used) afford o-aminobenzyl diketone derivatives 33, which readily undergo cyclization to 3-(trifluoroacetyl)indoles (Scheme 23, Table 2). 51 Thus, in this case, quinone imine serves as the source of indole nitrogen and phenyliodine(III) diacetate acts as the oxidant, and elimination of a water molecule is induced by trifluoroacetic acid.

This method has some substrate limitations. Thus it follows from Table 2 that in the presence of electron-withdrawing substituents in the benzene ring, indoles are rarely formed. An important practical application of the Nenitzescu reaction is the synthesis of tert-butyl 2-methyl-4-(trifluoromethyl)-1H-indole-3-carboxylate, a key intermediate in the synthesis of androgen receptor modulators. A convenient method has been developed for the synthesis of this indole from 4-amino-3-(trifluoromethyl)phenol and tert-butyl acetoacetate (Scheme 24). In 1968, Litell and Allen 52 showed that the Nenitzescu reaction of 2-trifluoromethyl-1,4-benzoquinone with 3-aminocrotonates leads to the selective formation of 4-substituted 5-hydroxyindoles. The regioselectivity is caused by the strong inductive effect of the trifluoromethyl group. Hence, the enamine addition occurs selectively at position 3 of benzoquinone.

Unlike the synthesis of 5-hydroxyindoles, the preparation of 6-hydroxyindoles is faced with some obstacles. Meanwhile, compounds of this class have found practical use as inhibitors of target proteins, hair colourant, cannabinoid receptor agonists (JWH-200, 34a) and fatty acid amide hydrolase (FAAH) inhibitors (34b); their fragments are encountered in indole alkaloid molecules (notoamide J, 34c). 53-56

The Nenitzescu synthesis is usually employed to prepare 5-hydroxyindole derivatives by the reaction of 1,4-benzoquinones with aminocrotonates. The Nenitzescu reaction with reversed regioselectivity resulting in 6-hydroxyindoles is encountered very rarely. This reaction, which depends on the type of substituent in the aminoacrylate, was discovered by Grinev and co-workers, 57 who replaced the N-alkyl derivatives by N-aryl derivatives (Scheme 25).

In 2017, an alternative protocol was proposed for the Nenitzescu reaction with reversed regioselectivity to prepare a broad range of 6-hydroxyindole derivatives without the use of catalysts. This method is suitable for preparing not

## 2.3. Ullmann reaction

N-Alkylated and N-arylated derivatives of methyl 1H-indole-3-carboxylate were synthesized for the first time by intramolecular Ullmann arylamination using CuI (Scheme 28). 59 This catalytic amination furnishes indole derivatives under mild reaction conditions in good to high yields. Under optimized conditions, this method was successfully used to prepare methyl 1H-indole-3-carboxylates with both aliphatic and aromatic substituents at the nitrogen atom (see Scheme 28). Attention is attracted by the possibility of preparing compounds with a chiral substituent at nitrogen, because the stereocentre of the chiral amine is not affected during the reaction.

This method was utilized for the synthesis of the simplest representative of N-isoprenylindole alkaloids, that is, methyl 1-(1,1-dimethylprop-2-en-1-yl)-1H-indole-3-carboxylate (40) (Scheme 29), isolated from Aporpium caryae basidiomycetes fungus. 60 This compound exhibits antifungal activity 61 and serves as the precursor for the synthesis of natural indole derivatives. Despite the seeming simplicity of structure 40, its synthesis is a non-trivial task because the direct introduction of the isoprenyl moiety into position 1 of indole is impossible. Methods for indirect synthesis of these compounds consist of numerous steps and give products in low overall yields. 62-64 The proposed method 60 for the synthesis of this alkaloid using the modified Ullmann reaction includes three steps, is based on the accessible

<!-- image -->

Indole derivatives can easily be prepared from commercially widely available 2,4,6-trinitrotoluene (TNT) (Scheme 34). 69 The first step is the nucleophilic substitution of the ortho-nitro group with a thiol to give the corresponding sulfide, which is then converted to enamine on treatment with dimethylformamide dimethylacetal. The enamine cyclizes to indole by the Leimgruber-Batcho method.

<!-- image -->

The formation of enamines 41 in the reaction of o-nitrophenylacetonitriles with the Vilsmeier reagent followed by the reduction with Zn in acetic acid affords various substituted 3-cyanoindoles 44 containing aryl, alkyl and aminoalkyl substituents in position 2 (Scheme 35, Table 3). 70 The Vilsmeier reagent is generated in situ before the reaction with nitroarene. 71 The reductive cyclization of cyanoenamines 41 affords 3-cyanoindoles 44, which contain the substituent from the starting amide in position 2. ## 2.5. Reissert indole synthesis

The first step of the classical Reissert indole synthesis comprises the condensation of o-nitrotoluene with diethyl oxalate to give ethyl o-nitrophenylpyruvic acid (Scheme 37). 72 The subsequent reduction of the nitro group leads to spontaneous cyclization giving esters of indole-2-carboxylic acid.

A modern version of the Reissert synthesis includes the catalytic hydrogenation in a continuous-flow reactor (H-Cube continuous-flow hydrogenation reactor) (Scheme 38, the yields are given according to the HPLC data with UV detection). 73 The best results were attained in the synthesis of 2-ethoxycarbonylindole (93% yield) at a hydrogen pressure of 1 bar in an ethyl acetate-ethanol mixture (1:1) at a flow rate of 1 ml min 71 and at 20 8 C.

## 2.6. Bartoli indole synthesis

The classical Bartoli indole synthesis involves the preparation of 7-substituted indoles by the reaction of ortho-substituted nitroarenes with vinyl Grignard reagents (Scheme 39). 74

Substituted nitroanilines were used for the first time instead of nitrobenzenes for the Bartoli indole synthesis; 75 this resulted in the development of a new route to substituted aminoindoles, promising building blocks for fine organic and pharmaceutical chemistry (Scheme 40). However, this approach was applicable only to a rather narrow range of substrates and Grignard reagents.

Mesylation and the subsequent intramolecular alkylation of alcohol 64 gives dihydropyrroloindole 65, which is an important structural moiety of potential medicinal agents and natural products (Scheme 57). 91 In addition, dihydropyrroloindoles can be prepared by cobalt-catalyzed intramolecular cyclization of 1-(but-3-enyl)-1H-indoles. 93

Recently, an unusual domino reaction has been proposed in which the same α-carbon atom of furan reacts, first, as a nucleophile and then as an electrophile. In the presence of acids, N-tosylfurfurylamine (66) reacts with 2-(tosylamino)benzyl alcohol giving rise to 2-(acylvinyl)indoles 67a-n (Scheme 58, Table 6). 94 The reaction proceeds as the Friedel-Crafts alkylation of furan position 2 followed by acid-catalyzed intramolecular attack by the ortho-amino group on the same atom.

The process optimization is mainly related to the choice of the acid. Three acidic agents, namely, AcOH-HCl (method a), CF3CO2H (method b) and CuSO4. 5 H2O (method c), were tested with a broad range of substrates.

Method a resulted in the lowest yields of indoles 67e, f, l. The use of primary benzyl alcohol gave a moderate yield of indole 67n. This can be attributed to lower stability of primary carbocations, which leads to oligomerization and, as a consequence, to resinification of the reaction mixture. The presence of electron-withdrawing substituents in the benzene ring in the para-position relative to the amino group substantially decreases the yields of indoles (products 67i, j).

The replacement of the leaving tosylamino group by the phthalimide group (furan 68) permits the synthesis of indoles 69 of another type owing to the amphiphilic nature of the C(2) atom (Scheme 59).

Indoles of both types (67a and 69a-c) are formed upon the reaction of N-furfurylbenzamides (Scheme 60). 94

The mechanism of formation of these indoles is depicted in Scheme 61. The results can be interpreted in terms of the competitive protonation of intermediate 70 either at the amide group or at the furan C(5) atom.

The replacement of the leaving tosylamino group by the phthalimide group (furan 68) permits the synthesis of indoles 69 of another type owing to the amphiphilic nature of the C(2) atom (Scheme 59).

Indoles of both types (67a and 69a-c) are formed upon the reaction of N-furfurylbenzamides (Scheme 60). 94

The mechanism of formation of these indoles is depicted in Scheme 61. The results can be interpreted in terms of the competitive protonation of intermediate 70 either at the amide group or at the furan C(5) atom. The protonation at the amide group (pathway a) substantially increases its nucleofugality (cation A), which leads to the formation of cation B upon amide elimination. The nucleophilic attack by the 2-tosylamino group on the furan C(2) atom followed by opening of the spiro ring in intermediate C generates indole 67. The protonation of the C(5) atom of furan (pathway b) affords cation D capable of generating spiro intermediate E, which is converted to intermediate F via tautomerization. It is noteworthy that in this case, too, the C(2) atom of furan demonstrates a dual reactivity, acting as a nucleophile in the step towards intermediate 70 and as an electrophile as the latter reacts with the 2-tosylamino group to yield intermediate E.

A simple and efficient synthesis of indoles 67 is based on thermolysis of 2-(2-azidobenzyl)furans with attack by the resulting nitrene on the ipso-position of the furan moiety (Scheme 62). 95 The cyclization is accompanied by furan ring opening and gives high yields of indoles with 2-acetylvinyl substituent at C(2), capable of further transformations.

Apparently, indole 67 is formed upon the electrophilic attack by the nitrene, generated via azide thermolysis, on furan position 2 to give spiro-zwitter-ion A (Scheme 63; shown for the substrate with R1 = H, R2 = Ph, R3 = Me). 95 Then the furan ring is opened, giving rise to 4-(3-phenyl-3H-indol-2-yl)but-3-en-2-one 71 and aromatization of the latter yields indole (Z)-67a, which isomerizes to the final indole (E)-67a.

A convenient method for the synthesis of 3,4-disubstituted 5-hydroxyindoles, which are used in synthetic and biological studies, consists in the following one-pot cascade of reactions: microwave-initiated intramolecular [4+2] cycloaddition of the alkynol group to furan, fragmentation and aromatization of the adduct and removal of the Boc protecting group. 96 The yields of indoles range from 15% to 74%; the presence of aromatic substituents provides higher yields. In earlier studies, 97 a sequence of reactions was developed, in which the 1,2-addition of lithiated derivative 72 to enone 73 affords allylic alcohol 74; microwave irradiation of this product gives tricyclic Diels-Alder adduct A; successive elimination of a water molecule and the protecting group gives indoles 75 via intermediates B and C (Scheme 64). If alkynone is taken instead of enone, the reaction follows a different pathway: cleavage of the oxygen bridge in adduct A affords structure D, aromatization of which in an early step yields phenol E. Elimination of one water molecule from E with simultaneous deprotection furnishes the target indole 76.

An interesting way of using a hydrogenated isoxazole analogue has been described. 99 First, attention is attracted by the preparation of substrates: in the presence of O2 and IPrCuCl [IPr is 1,3-bis(2,6-diisopropylphenyl)imidazol-2ylidene] (5 mol.%), [3+2]-cycloaddition of N-hydroxyallenylamines to nitrosobenzenes results in the desired 5-hydroxyisoxazolidines. Heating of this reaction mixture with DBU in toluene affords functionalized indoles (Scheme 67).

The mechanism of indole formation shown in Scheme 68 implies a key role of ketene imine intermediates: DBU promotes opening of the initial ketal to give intermediate A, which then undergoes catalytic rearrangement to the O-bonded isomer C via the intermediate formation of ketene imine species B. Unlike N-isomer A, intermediate C

actively undergoes the 3,3-rearrangement to give 2-aryl3-oxoamides D and E, which are finally converted to indoles.

An example of conversion of fused thiophene derivatives to indoles has been reported. The sulfone group in dibenzothiophene dioxides (e.g., compound 79), which are easily prepared by oxidation of dibenzothiophenes, undergoes, first, intermolecular and then intramolecular nucleophilic aromatic substitution with anilines, thus yielding carbazoles (Scheme 69). 100 This transformation of dibenzothiophenes to carbazoles does not require catalysis by heavy metals.

It is worth mentioning that the use of NaHMDS, instead of KHMDS, considerably decreases the product yield (for Rn = 4-Me, from 94% to 50%). The reaction proceeds equally smoothly for anilines with different types of substituents in the benzene ring and gives carbazoles in high yields. The reaction tolerates the vinyl group, although the corresponding carbazole is formed in a moderate yield. The TIPS group is partly hydrolyzed under the reaction conditions, resulting in a mixture of carbazoles with Rn = TIPSO and OH in 1:2.6 ratio. Unfortunately, unprotected p-aminophenol reacts to a low yield (26%).

The reaction was extended to the preparation of carbazoles substituted in the benzene rings and fused analogues (8 examples, 34% 94% yields) (see Scheme 69).

Among the five-membered heterocycles involved in the transformations to indoles, a special, rather peculiar, role is played by triazole derivatives (Scheme 71). 101 Formerly, the preparation of thiadiazine derivatives of type 81 from 4-amino-3-sulfanyl-4H-triazole (80) and subsequent trans-

formation with C7S (Ref. 102) and N7N (Ref. 103) bond cleavage have been reported. While analyzing reactions of this type, the authors arrived to the conclusion that the C7S bond cleavage can be used to synthesize 2-arylindoles.

The developed method serves as an example of indole synthesis based on a heterocyclic matrix. 4-Amino-3-sulfanyltriazole 80 was used as the matrix that was later separated during the workup of the reaction mixture. The reaction can be implemented as a one-pot procedure. Although the product yields are low, this method has benefits such as ready accessibility of the starting compounds and a simple experimental procedure.

Over the last years, only one example of transformation of six-membered heterocycles into indoles has been reported. 104 Readily accessible 3-alkoxycarbonyl-5-halo6-hydroxycoumarins 82 are converted to 4-halo-5-hydroxyindoles via a sequence of steps, in which the key step is the coumarin reduction to dihydrocoumarin 82 0 (the presence

es from the nitro group of the initial nitrostilbene rather than from triethyl phosphite. 88 The possible formation pathways of indoles and N-ethoxyindoles from labelled nitrostilbene 53* are shown in Scheme 54.

All of the proposed mechanisms start with deoxygenation of the nitro derivative to 2-nitrosostilbene 56*. The subsequent electrocyclization leads to the key intermediate 57*, which is reduced by an additional amount of triethyl phosphite yielding 2-arylindole 54. Alternatively, nitrosostilbene 56* can be subjected to further deoxygenation to give nitrene, which can be inserted into the alkene C7-H bond of stilbene to give indole 55. It is assumed that N-hydroxyindole 57* can be alkylated by triethyl phosphate generated in situ to give N-ethoxyindole 55*. The other possible mechanistic details include either the exchange of the hydroxy group in intermediate 57* with the ethoxy group to give non-labelled N-ethoxyindoles 55 or the exchange of labelled ethoxy group in compound 55* to give non-labelled indole 55. Since the experiment with oxygen-labelled nitrostilbene 53* affords labelled N-ethoxyindole, the mechanism can be depicted as shown in Scheme 55.

The electrocyclization of 2-nitrosostilbene intermediate 56a* (obtained from substrate 53a*) to bicyclic compound 58a* gives, after proton elimination, deprotonated N-hydroxyindole 59a*, which then serves as the substrate for alkylation with triethyl phosphate obtained upon deoxygenation of the nitro group. This alkylation yields oxygen-labelled N-ethoxyindole.

## 3. Transformations of other heterocycles

The strategy of transformation of one heterocycle into another is a potent and convenient means for the construction of a large diversity of heterocyclic structures. 89

The low resonance energy of the furan ring determines the ease of ring transformation giving various carbo- and heterocycles. 90 Of particular interest is ring transformation of easily accessible furan derivatives to indoles. An enormous role in the development of studies on the conversion of furans into indoles belongs to Professor A.V. Butin and his colleagues and followers.

Thus, the acid-catalyzed one-pot synthesis of indoles from o-aminobenzyl alcohols and furans has been developed (Scheme 56). 91 The method includes the in situ formation of aminobenzylfurans A and the subsequent ring transformation to afford indole 60 containing an ethyl ketone group in position 2. The method is scalable and tolerates a broad range of substrates and more functional groups than the two-step method developed previously. 92

The presence of this functional group in the indole bicyclic moiety implies the possibility of further transformations of indole into 2,3- and 1,2-fused indole derivatives and into indoles containing an a,b-unsaturated carbonyl moiety. The reduction of the carbonyl group of indole 60b (R1 = H, R2 = R3 = Ph) and the subsequent Friedel-Crafts cyclization give rise to tetracyclic indole 61. The removal of the tosyl protecting group of indole 60a (R1 = H, R2 = Ph, R3 = Me) on treatment with sodium naphthalenide affords NH-indole 62, which reacts with 2,3-dichloro-5,6-dicyano-1,4-benzoquinone (DDQ) to be

Scheme 54

converted to unsaturated ketone 63. Alternatively, after the reaction with excess Mg in methanol, the tosyl group in indole 60a is efficiently removed with simultaneous reduction of the carbonyl group to the hydroxy group. Mesylation and the subsequent intramolecular alkylation of alcohol 64 gives dihydropyrroloindole 65, which is an important structural moiety of potential medicinal agents and natural products (Scheme 57). 91 In addition, dihydropyrroloindoles can be prepared by cobalt-catalyzed intramolecular cyclization of 1-(but-3-enyl)-1H-indoles. 93

Recently, an unusual domino reaction has been proposed in which the same a-carbon atom of furan reacts, first, as a nucleophile and then as an electrophile. In the presence of acids, N-tosylfurfurylamine (66) reacts with 2-(tosylamino)benzyl alcohol giving rise to 2-(acylvinyl)indoles 67a-n (Scheme 58, Table 6). 94 The reaction proceeds as the Friedel-Crafts alkylation of furan position 2 followed by acid-catalyzed intramolecular attack by the ortho-amino group on the same atom.

The process optimization is mainly related to the choice of the acid. Three acidic agents, namely, AcOH-HCl (method a), CF3CO2H (method b) and CuSO4·5H2O (method c), were tested with a broad range of substrates.

Method a resulted in the lowest yields of indoles 67e, f, l. The use of primary benzyl alcohol gave a moderate yield of indole 67n. This can be attributed to lower stability of primary carbocations, which leads to oligomerization and, as a consequence, to resinification of the reaction mixture. The presence of electron-withdrawing substituents in the benzene ring in the para-position relative to the amino group substantially decreases the yields of indoles (products 67i, j).

The replacement of the leaving tosylamino group by the phthalimide group (furan 68) permits the synthesis of indoles 69 of another type owing to the amphiphilic nature of the C(2) atom (Scheme 59).

Indoles of both types (67a and 69a-c) are formed upon the reaction of N-furfurylbenzamides (Scheme 60). 94

The mechanism of formation of these indoles is depicted in Scheme 61. The results can be interpreted in terms of the competitive protonation of intermediate 70 either at the amide group or at the furan C(5) atom. The protonation at the amide group (pathway a) substantially increases its nucleofugality (cation A), which leads to the formation of cation B upon amide elimination. The nucleophilic attack by the 2-tosylamino group on the furan C(2) atom followed

## 4. Syntheses from o-alkynylanilines

The use of o-alkynylanilines is a part of a popular strategy for the synthesis of substituted indoles. o-Alkynylanilines are convenient substrates for annulation of a pyrrole ring to a benzene ring via intramolecular nucleophilic addition. 106 All methods of synthesis involving o-alkynylaniline derivatives can be split into several groups: (1) cyclization of ready o-alkynylaniline substrates under various conditions; (2) introduction of amine functions into the ortho-position of phenylacetylenes and their various uses; (3) utilization of the method to prepare functionalized indoles; (4) introduction of an ethynyl group into the ortho-position of the aniline molecule.

In the framework of the first group, a unique strategy has been developed for the preparation of substituted indoles by microwave-induced cycloisomerization of N-unsubstituted 2-alkynylanilines in water (Scheme 74). 107 The yields of the reaction products vary from moderate to

Using microwave radiation as an efficient activation method, one can obtain substituted indoles and azaindoles by the intramolecular cyclization of 2-alkynylanilines and alkynylpyridylamines (Scheme 75). 108 The cyclization proceeds with high yields and requires no transition metal-based catalysts.

The yields of reaction products virtually do not depend on the nature of substituent in the aromatic ring of alkynylamine. The highest yields were attained using 3-alkynyl4-aminopyridine. The yields of indoles and azaindoles sharply decrease if mere heating is used instead of microwave irradiation.

In the synthesis of 1,2-disubstituted indoles from 2-alkynylanilines and alkylboronic acids, copper acetate serves as the catalyst. 109

If the NH-acidity of the amine group is increased by introduction of an electron-withdrawing substituent to nitrogen, weak bases become suitable as catalysts for nucleophilic amination of the acetylene moiety. Thus, the cyclization of 2-ethynylN-sulfonylanilines to indoles smoothly proceeds in water in the presence of catalytic amounts of K2CO3 without transition metal catalysts to give products in high yields (Scheme 76, Table 7). 109 A possible reaction mechanism implies the K2CO3-induced formation of stabilized N-sulfonyl anion and nucleophilic addition of the anion to the triple bond.

Treatment of 2-phenylethynylaniline with ZnBr2 (1 equiv.) in refluxing toluene leads to 2-phenylindole in 91% yield. On the basis of calculations, the authors proposed a mechanism of intramolecular hydroamination of a model system, 2-(prop-1-ynyl)aniline, using ZnX2, which gives 2-methylindole. 110 Structural optimization identifies four stable intermediates: N-coordinated complex A, p-coordinated complex B, N- and p-coordinated complex C and s-complex D. The calculations confirm that complex C is the most stable one. Since the potential energy difference between complexes B and C is 16.8 kca mol 7 1, the reaction can proceed in the following way: ZnX2 reacts with 2-alkynylaniline to give complex C, which occurs in equilibrium with complex B, the latter cyclizes to afford s-complex D, which is protodemetallated to yield indole and ZnX2 (Scheme 79).

A simple and efficient synthesis of N-aryl-2-substituted indoles is based on the reaction of 2-alkynylanilines with diaryliodonium salts. 111

The intramolecular cyclization of o-aminophenylacetylenes to indoles catalyzed by gold compounds 112 and IrBr3 113 has been reported. The acetylene moiety can also

Scheme 79

<!-- image -->

be activated by the B(C6F5)3 catalyst. 114 The reaction of 2-alkynylanilines with B(C6F5)3 (15 mol.%) affords 2-substituted indoles in good to high yields via intramolecular hydroamination. The reaction follows only the 5endo-dig-cyclization pathway, the rate-limiting step of which is protodeboronation of intermediate A (Scheme 80).

The application of internal alkynes for indole synthesis by intramolecular hydroamination using FLP catalysis was demonstrated (FLP are frustrated Lewis pairs, that is, compounds containing both a Lewis acid and a Lewis base). The intramolecular hydroamination proceeds probably via the intermediate formation of a FLP-containing aniline derivative and borane followed by triple bond activation under the action of boron. The next step is 5endo-dig-cyclization to the zwitter-ionic indole derivative A (see Scheme 80), which undergoes protodeboronation giving the target indole and regenerating the borane catalyst.

The mechanism of indole formation shown in Scheme 68 implies a key role of ketene imine intermediates: DBU promotes opening of the initial ketal to give intermediate A, which then undergoes catalytic rearrangement to the O-bonded isomer C via the intermediate formation of ketene imine species B. Unlike N-isomer A, intermediate C actively undergoes the 3,3-rearrangement to give 2-aryl-3-oxoamides D and E, which are finally converted to indoles.

An example of conversion of fused thiophene derivatives to indoles has been reported. The sulfone group in dibenzothiophene dioxides (e.g., compound 79), which are easily prepared by oxidation of dibenzothiophenes, undergoes, first, intermolecular and then intramolecular nucleophilic aromatic substitution with anilines, thus yielding carbazoles (Scheme 69). 100 This transformation of dibenzothiophenes to carbazoles does not require catalysis by heavy metals.

It is worth mentioning that the use of NaHMDS, instead of KHMDS, considerably decreases the product yield (for Rn = 4-Me, from 94% to 50%). The reaction proceeds equally smoothly for anilines with different types of substituents in the benzene ring and gives carbazoles in high yields. The reaction tolerates the vinyl group, although the corresponding carbazole is formed in a moderate yield. The TIPS group is partly hydrolyzed under the reaction conditions, resulting in a mixture of carbazoles with Rn = TIPSO and OH in 1 : 2.6 ratio. Unfortunately, unprotected p-aminophenol reacts to a low yield (26%).

The acetylene moiety is also activated using N-iodosuccinimide (NIS). 116 A variety of 3-iodoindole derivatives were obtained in good to high yields from the corresponding 2-alkynylanilines by PPh3-catalyzed iodocyclization in the presence of N-iodosuccinimide under mild conditions (Scheme 81).

The presumptive mechanism of the reaction is depicted in Scheme 82. It is assumed that initially the NIS iodine atom is activated by Lewis base (PPh3) to give intermediate A. Then the electrophilic iodine atom adds to alkyne giving iodonium intermediate B, in which the intramolecular amination takes place to give the indole molecule.

Additional experiments were undertaken to establish the reaction mechanism; it was found that the presence of TEMPO [(2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidin-1-yl)oxyl] or oxygen completely inhibits the reaction. The authors assumed that DMSO is deprotonated on treatment with Bu t OK to give the anion. This anion gives off the electron to another DMSO molecule to generate radical A. Hydrogen abstraction from the substrate affords a-aminoalkyl radical B. The subsequent intramolecular radical cyclization gives intermediate C. After hydrogen abstraction from DMSO, the primary reaction product D is formed; then it undergoes allylic rearrangement to give the target indole (Scheme 85).

A similar C7 C bond formation is also observed with other N,N-dialkylo-alkynylanilines. 118 For example, a synthesis of 3-aroylindoles from N,N-dialkylo-alkynylanilines involves two C(sp3) 7 H bond activations, with tetra-n-butylammonium iodide (TBAI) as the catalyst and tert-butyl hydroperoxide (TBHP) as the oxidant (Scheme 86).

The ortho-isocyanate group was also used to synthesize 3-acylindoles by Pd-catalyzed insertion of this group into alkynes and its oxypalladation. 127,128

There are pathways for direct introduction of the amine group into the ortho-position relative to the alkynyl group in arylacetylene molecules, which can be catalyzed, in particular, by transition metal compounds.

A versatile and efficient method for the synthesis of diverse N-aminoindoles is based on Pd-catalyzed domino reactions of easily accessible 2-halophenylacetylenes with simple N,N-disubstituted hydrazines. 129 The reactions proceed in good to high yields, take several hours under mild conditions and are applicable to substrates with various functional groups (Scheme 96).

In another Pd-catalyzed process for introduction of the amino group into the aromatic ring, nitrogen comes from tert-butylsulfinamide (84), 130 which behaves as an equivalent of ammonia in the Pd-catalyzed amination of aryl bromides and chlorides. This reagent was used in the synthesis of indoles from 2-halophenols, with Pd-catalyzed cross-coupling of their triflates with acetylenes being the key step, followed by the replacement of the halogen atom by an amino group under the action of tert-butylsulfinamide (Scheme 97). The Pd(OAc)2-Xantphos [4,5-bis(diphenylphosphino)-9,9-dimethylxanthene] system served as the catalyst in both steps. The method is applicable for the preparation of indoles with sensitive functional groups such as ester and cyano groups or halogen atoms. The sulfinamide group is eliminated under

The Pd(OAc)2-Xantphos [4,5-bis(diphenylphosphino)-9,9-dimethylxanthene] system served as the catalyst in both steps. The method is applicable for the preparation of indoles with sensitive functional groups such as ester and cyano groups or halogen atoms. The sulfinamide group is eliminated under the reaction conditions to give the corresponding 2-aryl-NH-indoles in high yields.

The aromatic nucleophilic substitution of appropriate halogenated substrates can also be used to introduce the amino group into the ortho-position of arylacetylenes. For example, 2-substituted indoles and their aza analogues were obtained by the sequence of reactions including the aromatic nucleophilic substitution of a fluorine atom in o-alkynylfluorobenzene (-pyridine) by N-nucleophile (p-toluidine) and 5endo-dig-cyclization with the ortho-acetylene moiety, which plays a dual role, that is, the electron-withdrawing substituent activating the substrate towards the SNAr substitution and the C(1)7 C(2) group for the five-membered ring formation (Scheme 98). 131 The method is suitable for the construction of Ar7Nand N7C bonds in one synthetic step and gives indoles in moderate to good yields.

Original syntheses based on o-alkynylanilines are suitable for the direct preparation of unusual functionalized indoles.

It was found that the reaction of 2-phenylethynylaniline with acetone in the presence of [IrCp*Cl2]2 affords N-isopropenylindole rather than N-unsubstituted indole, the

The coordinated acetone reacts with imine derivative A, which leads to the final indole via the acetonecatalyzed proton transfer. The proposed mechanism is in good agreement with experimental data.

One more way of synthesis of functionalized indoles is associated with the intermediate preparation of organozinc derivatives. 134 When organozinc compounds are formed with two acidic protons of N-protected o-ethynylaniline [NH and C(sp)H] at 120 8 C, cyclization to 2,3-dizinc indole A takes place (Scheme 100). Since the formation of two C7Zn bonds is energetically favourable, the reaction proceeds smoothly and gives no by-products, although the reaction is actually endothermic for the free anionic form. The syntheses of functionalized indoles from o-aminophenylacetylenes are often associated with rearrangements and tandem or domino reactions. Indeed, in the case of NR1R2 o-alkynylanilines [R1 = Me, Et, Bn; R2 = C(O)R3, CO2R3, C(O)NHR3] and the PdCl2(MeCN)2 catalytic system, the functional substituent migrates to position 3 of the resulting indole. 135 A direct pathway to 2-aminoalkylindoles is the Cu-catalyzed three-component domino reaction of 2-ethynylaniline, secondary amines and aldehydes. 136 The direct ZnEt2-catalyzed anti-azacarboxylation of 2-alkynylanilines on treatment with CO2 yields indole-3-carboxylic acids. 137 The synthesis of indole-3-carboxamides from 2-ethynylanilines and isocyanates in the presence of a Rh catalyst proceeds as a cyclization-addition tandem reaction. 138, 139 An efficient synthesis of 3-aroylindoles from (o-dimethylaminophenyl)phenylacetylene with the Cu catalyst proceeds via activation of the C7H bond of the N-methyl group, intramolecular nucleophilic addition and tert-butyl hydroperoxide oxidation. 140

A selective synthesis of functionalized 3-allenyl-1H-indoles using tetra-n-butylammonium fluoride is a worthy alternative to the catalytic methods using noble metals (Scheme 102). 141 This approach, free from transition metal catalysts, is based on the efficiency of Bu4N+ and F7 ions in the reaction cascade including hydroamination of the carbon 7 carbon triple bond, elimination of the silyl protecting groups and site-selective sigmatropic [3,3]-aza Cope type rearrangement.

<!-- image -->

It is noteworthy that substrates with either electronwithdrawing (F, Cl, CF3) or electron-donating substituents (Me) in various positions can serve as equally convenient starting compounds for the proposed synthesis. The putative mechanism of the reaction is depicted in Scheme 103 in relation to the trimethylsilyl derivative.

Initially, TBAF participates in hydroamination with simultaneous removal of the silyl protection (Bu4N+ ion is stabilized by the originating acetylene carbanion), resulting, after C(3) protonation, in the indolium intermediate A, which then undergoes sigmatropic [3,3]-aza Cope rearrangement to partially dearomatized intermediate B; proton migration leads to aromatization and the subsequent removal of the silyl protection gives the target indole.

The preparation of indole derivatives with a boron-containing substituent in position 3 from o-alkynylanilines has been reported. 142 This study describes a convenient

The reaction is accompanied by N7B bond cleavage and N7C and B7C bond formation, which gives, in one step, borylated indoles suitable for further functionalization.

A new method for the synthesis of 3-phosphinylindole derivatives is based on the Ag-catalyzed cycloaddition of 2-alkynylN-tosylanilines on treatment with H-phosphine oxides. 143

The putative mechanism of the process is summarized in Scheme 106. The cross-coupling affords N,N-dialkyl2-(1-alkynyl)aniline A. Aryl iodide is added after completion of the cross-coupling. The oxidative addition of aryl iodide to Pd0 yields the electrophilic intermediate ArPdI, which activates the triple bond of structure A via coordination, thus giving palladium p-complex B. The latter immediately undergoes the intramolecular transamination according to the 5endo-dig-cyclization pattern to afford indolium intermediate C, which eliminates the methyl group as a result of SN2 substitution by the in situ generated iodide anion, thus yielding indole-containing PdII intermediate D.

The oxidative addition of aryl iodide to Pd0 yields the electrophilic intermediate ArPdI, which activates the triple bond of structure A via coordination, thus giving palladium p-complex B. A convenient one-pot regioselective synthesis of 2,3-disubstituted indoles from 2-bromoanilines is based on successive Pd-catalyzed Sonogashira cross-coupling with alkylacetylenes, amidopalladation and reductive elimination. 145

A rhodium-catalyzed introduction of an alkynyl group into the ortho-position of phenylhydrazines accompanied by in situ indole formation has been reported. 146

In an interesting synthetic route to 2-haloindole-3-carboxylic acids, instead of the ethynyl group, the dihalovinyl group was employed as the ethynyl group precursor (Scheme 107). 147 The optimal conditions of synthesis of 2-halo-3-carboxyindoles from 2-(2,2-dihalovinyl)anilines include heating of the substrates in DMSO at 120 8C in the presence of caesium carbonate for several minutes. However, the reaction can successfully proceed over a broad temperature range in aqueous media and can be conducted using various combinations of a polar solvent and a carbonate base; the only limiting factor is the substrate solubility. Substituents present in 2-(2,2-dihalovinyl)anilines are, most often, tolerant to the reaction conditions and the product yields are usually high. The reaction mechanism includes the initial formation of the alkynyl bromide intermediate followed by cyclization and addition of carbon dioxide. Due to the nature of the intermediate, we attributed the cited publication to the Section devoted to the use of o-alkynylanilines in indole synthesis.

In some tandem methods, the reductive cyclization to indoles is preceded by the formation of a substituted vinyl group in the ortho-position to the nitro group. For example, the Vilsmeier-Haack formylation of 4-(2-aryl-2-oxoethyl)5-nitrophthalonitriles followed by reduction gives rise to 3-acyl-1-hydroxy-1H-indole-5,6-dinitriles (Scheme 113). 158

## 8.2. C 7 Nbond formation

Most often, the C 7 N bond formation in the indole synthesis occurs with the participation of anilines with an appropriate functional group in the ortho -position to the amino group. An example of such groups is vinyl, which undergoes the C 7 H amination followed by oxidation to give a pyrrole ring.

Efficient intramolecular C 7 H amination in N -tosyl2-alkenylanilines requiring no transition metal-based catalysts and using DDQ as the oxidant was utilized for the synthesis of a broad range of substituted indoles (Scheme 147). 206 The mechanism of this process includes the generation of a radical cation via single-electron transfer (SET) and migration steps in the intermediate phenonium ion. Apart from the absence of metal catalysts, benefits of the method include experimental simplicity and large diversity of suitable substrates.

## Scheme 147

R 1 =H, Me, OMe, NO2, Cl; R 2 , R 3 , R 4 =H, Alk, Ar, Het

The effect of substituents on the reaction efficiency was studied for quite a number of substrates with R 3 =H (Table 15). N -Tosylstyrylanilines (R 4 =Ar) are readily C 7 H-aminated yielding the corresponding indoles in good to high yields irrespective of the nature of the aryl substituent, with only minor electronic and/or steric require- ments (rows 1 - 10 in Table 15). The Z -isomer also reacts to give the product in a yield of 76% (row 2). Alkenes with naphthyl and hetaryl substituents are also suitable for this reaction (rows 11, 12). It is noteworthy that the presence of the aryl substituent with the electron-donating 4-MeO group (row 6) results in a mixture of 2- and 3-substituted indoles in 5.6 : 1 ratio, which attests to occurrence of migration steps in the carbocation intermediate.

A highly regioselective synthesis of 3-arylindoles was performed starting from b -aryl-substituted 2-styrylanilines, with FeCl3 and DDQ as the cheap and available catalyst and oxidant being used together (Scheme 148); 207 meanwhile the employment of DDQ alone resulted in the formation of isomeric 2-arylindoles. 163 Attention is attracted by the unusual joint effect of the components of this system on the 1,2-migration of the aryl group.

It can be seen that electronwithdrawing substituents in the aryl moiety (NO2 and CF3) completely suppress its migration from position 2 to 3. Conversely, the electronic properties of substituents in the indole benzene ring have virtually no effect on the regioselectivity of the reaction.

## PERSPECTIVE

## Recent advances in indole syntheses: New routes for a classic target

## Rub´ en Vicente*

Received 12th May 2011, Accepted 22nd June 2011 DOI: 10.1039/c1ob05750b

Indoles are the most abundant heterocycles in biologically active natural products, pharmaceuticals, agrochemicals, and are relevant substructures in functional materials. As a result, the development of methodologies that enable the synthesis of these compounds is continuously demanded. This review summarizes most recent and relevant approaches towards the preparation of this prevalent structural motif.

## Introduction

The indole ring is one of the most widely distributed heterocycles in nature. Although indole was isolated for the first time by Baeyer from the treatment of indigo with oleum, 1 in chemistry grew impressively from the 1950s, when several structurally diverse indole derivatives were found to have significant biological activities. As a result, the indole moiety appears as a

Fig. 1 Selected examples of relevant indole derivatives.

<!-- image -->

The great attention in organic chemistry that indole preparation has attracted, has turned into a large number of different synthetic approaches. Thus, while traditional approaches are based on condensation and cyclization sequences, 3 transition metal-catalyzed C-C- and C-N-bond formation reactions have recently enabled the development of alternative methodologies towards modular indole syntheses. 4,5 Despite the plentiful collection of available protocols to prepare indoles, the enormous interest in the indole structure results in this research area being continuously active. This review summarizes the most relevant achievements of the last five years in this important research area and emphasizes processes which involve a conceptual novelty in the synthesis of indoles.

## Indole syntheses from o-alkynylanilines and derivatives

Indole syntheses from o-alkynylaniline derivatives have become very popular during the last decade. These reactions included base6 or ammonium fluoride-mediated cyclizations 7 and

Downloaded by Brown University on 05 December 2012

Scheme 1 Indole synthesis via cyclization of o-alkynylanilines.

<!-- image -->

transition metal-catalyzed reactions (Scheme 1). Simple cyclization reactions of o-alkynylaniline proved feasible by using a variety of metal catalysts, 8 however these reactions often led to unsubstituted indoles at position C-3.

In this section, relevant selected examples, which include the functionalization at position C-3 are presented. Thus, an electrophilic iodonium-mediated cyclization which led to N-H-free 3-iodoindole derivatives was reported by Barluenga using IPy2BF4 as an iodonium source (Scheme 2). 9,10 The formation of indole 8 was proposed to occur through an initial interaction of electrophilic iodine with alkyne 6, which led to intermediate 7. A nucleophilic attack by nitrogen would lead to indole 8. HBF4 likely activated the reagent in this reaction, while pyridine would facilitate proton abstraction during the transformation of 7 into indole 8.

Scheme 2 IPy2BF4-promoted synthesis of 3-iodoindole 8.

<!-- image -->

These valuable compounds could subsequently be functionalized into complex molecules using well-known organometallic chemistry. Related cyclizations which made use of copper halides enabled the preparation of 3-bromo- or 3-chloro-indoles; however, a large excess of the metal salt was required. 11

In recent years, a variety of palladium-catalyzed cyclizations of o-alkynylaniline derivatives which permitted the preparation of 3substituted indoles was reported. For instance, Cacchi reported the preparation of 3-arylindole 13 from aniline 9 in a straightforward manner through a sequence comprising an aminopalladation and reductive elimination. 12 The process would be initiated by an oxidative addition to aryl halides or arenediazonium salts such as 10 (Scheme 3). 13 Coordination of the arylpalladium to the alkyne

Scheme 3 Palladium-catalyzed synthesis of 3-arylindole 13 using arenediazonium salt 10 .

<!-- image -->

|

likely triggered a nucleophilic attack and a subsequent reductive elimination led to the corresponding N-H-free indole.

Formation of 3-indolylpalladium intermediates similar to 11 has been further exploited in order to introduce other valuab

aniline derivatives which permitted the preparation of 3-substituted indoles was reported. For instance, Cacchi reported the preparation of 3-arylindole 13 from aniline 9 in a straightforward manner through a sequence comprising an aminopalladation and reductive elimination. 12 The process would be initiated by an oxidative addition to aryl halides or arenediazonium salts such as 10 (Scheme 3). 13 Coordination of the arylpalladium to the alkyne

Scheme 3 Palladium-catalyzed synthesis of 3-arylindole 13 using arenediazonium salt 10 .

<!-- image -->

likely triggered a nucleophilic attack and a subsequent reductive elimination led to the corresponding N-H-free indole.

Formation of 3-indolylpalladium intermediates similar to 11 has been further exploited in order to introduce other valuable functionalities. 14 In this sense, de Lera reported very recently the preparation of alkenyl-substituted indoles combining a palladium-catalyzed cyclization with an oxidative Heck-coupling. 15 Thus, in the presence of acrylate 15 , aniline 14 could be converted into 3alkenylindole 18 in good yield under aerobic reaction conditions using PdCl2 as catalyst (Scheme 4). A plausible mechanism could start with an aminopalladation, followed by an alkene insertion to generate intermediate 17 . Indole 18 was obtained after b-hydride elimination. The presence of O2 likely enabled the regeneration of an active PdX2 species through oxidation. Interestingly, b-hydride elimination in intermediates such as 17 could be avoided using LiBr, affording the corresponding 3-alkylindoles. 16

Scheme 4 Palladium-catalyzed synthesis of 3-alkenylindole 18 .

<!-- image -->

The replacement of an olefin by an aldehyde was further developed by Lu. 17 In this case, a cationic palladium catalyst allowed an efficient preparation of 3-hydroxymethylindole 23 using tosyl-protected aniline 19 and ethylglyoxalate (20) (Scheme 5). It is noteworthy that this protocol tolerated the presence of a C-Cl bond, which could be further manipulated. This tandem reaction involved an addition of intermediate 21 to the carbonyl group to generate palladium alkoxide 22 . A simple protonolysis afforded indole 23 and regenerated a catalytically competent Pd(II) species, avoiding the necessity of additional oxidants.

Scheme 5 Palladium-catalyzed synthesis of 3-hydroxymethylindole 23 .

<!-- image -->

An important drawback of the above mentioned methodologies lies in the preparation of the o-alkynylanilines. A practical protocol which circumvents this inconvenience was reported by Lu. 18 Hence, 2,3-disubstituted indole 29 was prepared in excellent yield through a one-pot three-component domino Sonogashira-cyclization-coupling sequence starting from o-iodoaniline 24 , which did not require the isolation of the o-alkynylaniline 27 (Scheme 6).

Scheme 6 Palladium-catalyzed multicomponent synthesis of 2,3-disubstituted indole 29 .

<!-- image -->

An interesting cascade multicomponent synthesis of 2(aminomethyl)indoles was recently described by Ohno. 19 This protocol made use of N-protected o-alkynylanilines, aldehydes and secondary amines, which were selectively coupled using inexpensive CuBr as catalyst. In this manner, indole 35 was prepared in very good yield (Scheme 7). The reaction likely proceeded via Mannich-type coupling followed by a copper-catalyzed cyclization.

Scheme 7 Copper-catalyzed multicomponent synthesis of 2-(aminomethyl)indole 35 .

<!-- image -->

Biologically relevant 3-(2-aminoethyl)-1H-indol-2-amine derivatives were prepared by Wu through a copper-catalyzed three-component reaction of o-alkynylanilines, sulfonyl azide and nitroalkenes (Scheme 8). 20 Hence, Boc-protected o-alkynylaniline 36 was converted into substituted indole 41 under mild reaction conditions. The process was proposed to start with the formation of a triazolyl-copper species 38 , which evolved into intermediate 39 via N2 extrusion and copper migration. A subsequent intramolecular cyclization led to 2-aminoindole 40 . Finally, an intermolecular Michael addition to alkene 37 and tautomerization would afford indole 41 .

N-(2-Alkynylphenyl)lactams could easily be converted into fused indoles as reported by Zhang (Scheme 9). 21 Thus, the thermal treatment of lactam 42 with PtCl4 as catalyst under an atmosphere of O2 selectively yielded indole 45 . The reaction was initiated by an aminometallation which led to metal-ammonium ylide 43 . Then, two consecutive migration reactions took place. Firstly, a 1,2-acyl migration gave rise to platinum-carbene intermediate 44 , which

Scheme 8 Copper-catalyzed multicomponent synthesis of indole 41 .

<!-- image -->

Scheme 9 Platinum-catalyzed synthesis of fused indole 45 .

<!-- image -->

finally underwent 1,2-alkyl migration to yield the corresponding indole 45 . A related process occurred when employing amines instead of amides as starting materials and rhenium or tungsten complexes as catalysts. 22

A related platinum carbene was generated in situ from propargyl ether 46 through an alkyne activation as described by Iwasawa (Scheme 10). 23 Here, platinum carbene species 49 resulted from a cyclization/elimination sequence through intermediate 48 , and was subsequently trapped in the presence of an excess of vinyl ether 47 . Thus, this protocol enabled the preparation of fused indole 51 in good yield.

## Indole syntheses from o-haloanilines

Since its first report in 1991, the Larock indole synthesis has become one of the most attractive and practical method for the preparation of 2,3-disubstituted indoles, 24 and it has been extensively studied. 4 g, h This protocol consists of a palladium-catalyzed 25 heteroannulation of internal alkynes with N-protected o-iodoanilines (Scheme 11), however, alternatives which enable the use of o-bromo- or o-chloroanilines as well as halobenzoic acids as precursors have been developed. 26 A major drawback of this protocol arises in the control of the regioselectivity when unsymmetrical alkynes are used. Normally, the more sterically hindered group is attached at position C-2. Nevertheless, with similarly substituted alkynes, mixtures of regioisomers are obtained.

Scheme 11 Larock indole synthesis.

<!-- image -->

On the contrary, intramolecular versions of the Larock indole synthesis give rise to regioselectively substituted indoles. 27 The importance and validity of this approach becomes apparent in the next example. Here, Boger made use of the Larock's procedure to prepare complex indole 53 (Scheme 12), which is a key intermediate in the synthesis of relevant natural products such as chloropeptines. 28 Although a stoichiometric amount of Pd(OAc)2 was required, the impressive yield obtained is noteworthy considering the dense functionalization of the starting aniline 52.

Scheme 12 Synthesis of densely functionalized indole 53 using Larock's procedure.

<!-- image -->

A different approach to indoles using o-chloroanilines and internal alkynes was described by Ackermann (Scheme 13). 29 This protocol comprised a multicatalytic sequence. For instance, indole 57 was obtained starting with a highly regioselective titanium-catalyzed hydroamination of unsymmetrical alkyne 55

Scheme 13 Synthesis of indole 57 via titanium-catalyzed hydroamination/palladium-catalyzed Heck-coupling.

<!-- image -->

with aniline 54, followed by a subsequent palladium-catalyzed intramolecular Heck-coupling in intermediate 56. The best results for the Heck reaction were obtained when using electron-rich PCy3 or sterically hindered imidazolium salts as preligands. Moreover, this sequential procedure could be achieved using ruthenium-catalyzed hydroaminations as well. 30

Barluenga described an alternative approach to regioselectively access indoles from o-haloanilines using alkenyl bromides as coupling partners. 31 Here, a catalytic system comprising Pd2(dba)3 and electron-rich phosphine DavePhos enabled the synthesis of 2-substituted indole 61 from aniline 58 and a-bromostyrene (59) (Scheme 14). Interestingly, the reaction proceeded exclusively through the cleavage of the C-Br-bond, while the C-Cl-bond remained unaltered. This cascade reaction started with a Buchwald-Hartwig-type C-N-bond formation giving rise to intermediate 60, which underwent an intramolecular Heck-coupling. Alternatively, the use of trans-1,2-bromoalkene derivatives afforded the corresponding 3-substituted indoles.

Scheme 14 Palladium-catalyzed synthesis of indole 61 via C-N-bond formation/Heck coupling sequence.

<!-- image -->

## Indole syntheses from o-alkynylhaloarenes or o-dihaloarenes

In contrast to well studied approaches to indole syntheses from aniline derivatives, the use of dihaloarenes has received far less attention. However, the development of efficient and versatile transition metal-catalyzed C-N-bond formation processes through Buchwald-Hartwig or Ullman-Goldberg-type couplings has enabled the establishment of new methodologies to prepare indoles from o-alkynylhaloarenes or dihaloarenes and amines. The most relevant examples regarding the use of these starting materials are discussed in this section.

A catalytic two-step one-pot procedure for the synthesis of 2-substituted indoles using o-alkynylchloroarenes was described by Doye. 32 The combination of a titanium-catalyzed hydroamination of alkyne 62 with sterically hindered amine 63, followed by a subsequent palladium-catalyzed N-arylation of the enamine 65 afforded indole 66 in good yield (Scheme 15). Interestingly, fused indoles could be assembled with an intramolecular version of this protocol.

Remarkably, this overall transformation could be achieved with a single catalyst, as reported by Ackermann. 33 Thus, the sole use of palladium or low-priced copper 33 a, 34 catalysts enabled the accomplishment of this transformation with superior efficiency in terms of yields and shortening reaction times. In some cases, milder bases such as K3PO4 could be employed, 35 thereby allowing

Scheme 15 One-pot hydroamination/C-N-bond formation synthesis of indole 66. (L = 1,3-bis(2,4,6-trimethylphenyl)imidazolium chloride).

<!-- image -->

a better functional group tolerance. In addition, Ackermann found that a Sonogashira-coupling reaction was compatible with the hydroamination/amination sequence. 33 This overall transformation simplified the preparation of indole 69, which could be accessed directly from o-dihaloarene 67 by a sequential treatment with alkyne 25 and p-toluidine (68) (Scheme 16).

Scheme 16 One-pot synthesis of indole 69 from o-dihaloarene 67 (L = 1,3-bis(2,4,6-trimethylphenyl)imidazolium chloride).

<!-- image -->

A novel route to indole synthesis using o-dihaloarenes and imines was devised by Barluenga. 36 In this methodology an a-deprotonation of the imine set the stage to allow two consecutive palladium-catalyzed cross-coupling processes. Thus, under basic conditions, imine 71 was a-arylated with unsymmetric dihaloarene 70 to yield intermediate imine

n found that a Sonogashira-coupling reaction was compatible with the hydroamination/amination sequence. 33 This overall transformation simplified the preparation of indole 69, which could be accessed directly from o-dihaloarene 67 by a sequential treatment with alkyne 25 and p-toluidine (68) (Scheme 16).

**Scheme 16** One-pot synthesis of indole 69 from o-dihaloarene 67 (L = 1,3bis(2,4,6-trimethylphenyl)imidazolium chloride).

<!-- image -->

A novel route to indole synthesis using o-dihaloarenes and imines was devised by Barluenga. 36 In this methodology an a-deprotonation of the imine set the stage to allow two consecutive palladium-catalyzed cross-coupling processes. Thus, under basic conditions, imine 71 was a-arylated with unsymmetric dihaloarene 70 to yield intermediate imine 72, which tautomerized to the corresponding enamine 73. A subsequent BuchwaldHartwig amination gave rise to 2,3-disubstituted indole 74 in good yield (Scheme 17). Remarkably, the regioselectivity of the overall transformation could be controlled according to the preference of the oxidative addition to the C-Br- over the C-Cl-bond.

**Scheme 17** Synthesis of indole 74 via palladium-catalyzed imine a-arylation/N-arylation sequence.

<!-- image -->

This reaction was found to proceed regioselectively and with the same efficiency using o-chloroaryl sulfonates, which could be prepared from readily available inexpensive phenols. 37 Moreover, a convenient 'on water' microwave-promoted protocol of this transformation was developed as well. 38 Similarly, Barluenga found that indoles could be built in a modular three-component cascade reaction employing simple starting materials such as bromoalkenes, primary amines and o-dihaloarenes. 36 In this manner, indole 79 was efficiently prepared from b-bromostyrene (59), aniline (75) and 1,2-dichlorobenzene (76) (Scheme 18). The overall transformation consisted of an alkenyl amination, an a-arylation of the imine and finally an intramolecular N-arylation, which were promoted by a single palladium catalyst. The selectivity of this process is noteworthy, since the three different cross-coupling events took place consecutively and with complete chemoselectivity.

**Scheme 18** Palladium-catalyzed three-component synthesis of indole 79.

<!-- image -->

Jørgensen made use of well-established cross-coupling protocols to develop an elegant indole synthesis from o-dihaloarenes and allyl amines. 39 Here, a catalytic system comprising Pd(OAc)2 and dppf as ligand enabled the regioselective synthesis of indole 83 from haloarene 80 and allyl amine (81) (Scheme 19). The sequence was proposed to start with an N-arylation. A Heck-coupling on intermediate 82 and an isomerization led to the indole in a good yield. Later, this protocol was extended to the use of chloroaryl triflates and Boc-protected allyl amines. 40

**Scheme 19** Synthesis of indole 83 from o-dihaloarene 80 and allyl amine (81).

<!-- image -->

## Indole syntheses from b-(pseudo)halostyrene derivatives

A variety of indole derivatives were prepared by Lautens and Alper when using ortho-gem-dihalovinylanilines as substrates. 41 This palladium-catalyzed reaction proceeded through an intramolecular Buchwald-Hartwig-coupling giving rise to 2-haloindoles. These valuable substrates could be further functionalized through

Downloaded by Brown University on 05 December 2012

inter- or intramolecular C-C-couplings enabling the development of synthetically useful sequences. For instance, Florent recently developed a domino coupling reaction for the synthesis of 2-acylindole derivatives. 42 With this approach, indole 87 was efficiently prepared from aniline 84 and arylboronic acid 85 when the reaction was conducted under a high pressure of CO (Scheme 20). This one-pot palladium-catalyzed reaction occurred via sequential C-N-bond formation, carbonylation and Suzuki-coupling. A remarkable feature of this protocol resided in the use of a mild base. In this manner, sensitive moieties were well tolerated.

**Scheme 20** Synthesis of 2-acylindole 87 from dihalovinylaniline 84.

<!-- image -->

Alternatively, Willis reported a related palladium-catalyzed indole synthesis employing o-halostyryl triflates 43 or halides 44 as starting materials. In this approach, two consecutive C-N-bonds were formed and allowed for the introduction of functionalities at the N-atom. For instance, indole 91 bearing a bulky substituent at position 1, was synthesized from b-chlorostyrene 88 and tert-amylamine (89) in good yield. The low Z:E ratio of the starting material, in contrast with the obtained yield, indicated that both geometrical isomers could be converted into the desired indole, thereby, simplifying the preparation of the starting substrate (Scheme 21). 44c

**Scheme 21** Synthesis of indole 91 from o-bromob-chlorostyrene 88.

## Indole syntheses from o-propargylanilines

As presented above, the indole core could be easily assembled through metal-catalyzed 5endo-dig cyclizations from o-alkynylanilines. On the contrary, Malacria studied the feasibility to prepare indole derivatives via a 5exo-dig cyclization using o-propargylanilines as substrates. 45 Thus, a straightforward preparation of 3-alkoxyindole 95 was achieved from aniline 92 using PtCl2

|

as catalyst. Moreover, it was found that an excess of simple acids such as SiO2 or p-toluenesulfonic acid promoted the transformation as well (Scheme 22). A mechanistic proposal for the Brønsted acid-promoted reaction would start with an alkyne activation by the acid, which triggered the cyclization to generate ammonium intermediate 93. Then, a facilitated 3-aza-Cope rearrangement towards immonium species 94 and subsequent aromatization likely afforded the final product.

**Scheme 22** Brønsted acid-promoted cyclization of aniline 92.

<!-- image -->

Moreover, it was found that an excess of simple acids such as SiO2 or p-toluenesulfonic acid promoted the transformation as well (Scheme 22). A mechanistic proposal for the Brønsted acid-promoted reaction would start with an alkyne activation by the acid, which triggered the cyclization to generate ammonium intermediate 93. Then, a facilitated 3-aza-Cope rearrangement towards immonium species 94 and subsequent aromatization likely afforded the final product.

Scheme 22 Brønsted acid-promoted cyclization of aniline 92.

In a related report, Chan described a gold-catalyzed 5exo-dig cyclization of tertiary propargyl alcohols. 46 In this study, a variety of differently substituted indoles could be obtained, however, their structures had a strong dependence on the substrate. For example, alkynol 96 was converted into 2-vinylindole 99 in the presence of HMPA using an in situ generated cationic gold catalyst (Scheme 23). The formation of indole 99 took place through a gold-promoted cyclization, which afforded ammonium species 97. Subsequent proton abstraction gave rise to alcohol 98, which underwent a dehydration efficiently yielding indole 99. Despite its beneficial influence, the role of the HPMA remained unclear at this stage.

## Indole syntheses from acetophenone derivatives

As was shown in the previous sections, anilines or haloarenes bearing an alkenyl or alkynyl substituent at the ortho position have become the trendiest substrates for the synthesis of indoles. In contrast, Pei disclosed an attractive alternative to access 2-substituted indole derivatives using o-aminochloroacetophenones and Grignard reagents, which did not require the presence of transition metal catalysts. 47 In this manner, the treatment of chloroketone 100 with alkynyl Grignard 101 under very mild

Downloaded by Brown University on 05 December 2012

reaction conditions afforded indole 104 in a reasonable yield (Scheme 24). The reaction was likely started with the addition of the organometallic reagent. Then, a key step consisting of a [1,2]-aryl migration in intermediate 102 took place, affording new ketone 103, which gave rise to indole 104 through an intramolecular condensation. 48

Scheme 24 Synthesis of indole 104 from chloroketone 100.

<!-- image -->

A copper-catalyzed synthesis of indole-2-carboxylic acid esters from 2-haloarylketones and isocyanoacetates was reported by Cai. 49 Although the use of 2-bromoarenes yielded the desired indoles in good yields, it is remarkable that this ligand-free protocol could be efficiently achieved with less reactive 2-chloroketone 105 (Scheme 25). A sequence involving a condensation/intramolecular C-N-bond formation/deformylation likely operated to assemble the indole core.

Scheme 25 Copper-catalyzed synthesis of indole 107.

<!-- image -->

A different ligand-free copper-catalyzed indole synthesis comprising the use of terminal alkynes and hydrazones derived from 2-aminobenzaldehydes was very recently described by Wang. 50 Thus, 2-substituted indole 112 could be prepared from hydrazone 108 and alkyne 25, using CuBr as catalyst and Cs2CO3 as a mild base (Scheme 26). A mechanistic proposal would involve a coupling of the alkyne with an in situ generated carbene to give rise to intermediate 110, which could evolve via isomerization/cyclization to yield indole 112. Unfortunately, the scope of this transformation seems still limited, since only very few examples have been reported.

## Indole syntheses from arylhydrazines and related compounds

The Fischer indole synthesis has remained as one of the essential methodologies for the synthesis of indole derivatives. This approach consists of the condensation of an aromatic hydrazine with a ketone, followed by a [3,3]-sigmatropic rearrangement, subsequent ammonia elimination and aromatization (Fig. 2, a). 51 A mechanistically related process, which could be started with an alkyne hydroamination reaction constitutes an interesting alternative route to the indole scaffold (Fig. 2, b).

Scheme 26 Copper-catalyzed coupling of hydrazone 108 with alkyne 25.

<!-- image -->

Fig. 2 Fischer indole synthesis vs. alkyne hydroamination-based indole synthesis.

<!-- image -->

Pioneering studies of this interesting approach were accomplished by Bergman, where zirconium-mediated domino hydroamination-Fischer-type cyclizations were reported. 52 Later, catalytic procedures using titanium catalysts and zinc salts as additives were developed by Odom and others. 53 Despite the interest of this reaction, these protocols unfortunately presented some drawbacks in terms of the sensitivity of functional groups towards titanium, the necessity to protect the hydrazine or low regioselectivities.

Some of these disadvantages were elegantly overcome by Beller. 54 Thus, it was found that titanium was not required to promote the hydroamination step. Thereby, zinc acting as Lewis acid was apparently inducing both the hydroamination and the subsequent cyclization. This easy protocol enabled the preparation of a variety of indoles from readily available reagents. For instance, zinc-mediated reaction of commercially accessible hydrazine 113 with terminal alkyne 114 afforded hydroxy-functionalized indole 116 with complete Markovnikov regioselectivity (Scheme 27).

Scheme 27 Zinc-mediated hydroamination-Fischer cyclization.

<!-- image -->

A different pathway to access an enamine intermediate such as 115 in indole synthesis was shrewdly designed by Knochel. 55 In this approach hydrazines were replaced by aryldiazonium salts, which are readily available reagents. Moreover, the use of alkynes as coupling partners was substituted by alkylzinc reagents, which conveniently tolerated a variety of valuable functional groups. As an example,

uent cyclization. This easy protocol enabled the preparation of a variety of indoles from readily available reagents. For instance, zinc-mediated reaction of commercially accessible hydrazine 113 with terminal alkyne 114 afforded hydroxy-functionalized indole 116 with complete Markovnikov regioselectivity (Scheme 27).

Scheme 27 Zinc-mediated hydroamination-Fischer cyclization.

<!-- image -->

A different pathway to access an enamine intermediate such as 115 in indole synthesis was shrewdly designed by Knochel. 55 In this approach hydrazines were replaced by aryldiazonium salts, which are readily available reagents. Moreover, the use of alkynes as coupling partners was substituted by alkylzinc reagents, which conveniently tolerated a variety of valuable functional groups. As an example, functionalized indole 121 was prepared in excellent yield and with complete regioselectivity from diazonium salt 117 and alkylzinc complex 118 (Scheme 28). Thus, azo compound intermediate 119 was formed by coupling of substrates 117 and 118. Then, an isomerization to enamine 120 took place, which was capable of undergoing cyclization as in the traditional Fischer indole protocol. Interestingly, this protocol was applied to the preparation of the anti-inflammatory drug indomethacin (5) (see Fig. 1).

Scheme 28 Synthesis of indole 121 using diazonium salt 117 and organozinc 118.

<!-- image -->

## Indole syntheses via nitrene or carbene insertions

Modern developments towards indole syntheses make use of well-established C-N-bond formation protocols through crosscoupling reactions. These amination reactions are performed with aniline or amine derivatives. Nevertheless, less conventional sources of nitrogen, such as nitrenes have been successfully employed in indole synthesis.

Nitrenes can be generated in situ by rearrangement of 2H-azirines. When bearing an aryl substituent at position C-2, a nitrene intermediate could undergo an intramolecular C-H insertion yielding the corresponding indole. 56 Thus, Zheng recently described the preparation of, among others, 2,3-disubstituted indole 125. 57 This reaction took place through a ring opening of azirine 122 and the subsequent formation of iron-nitrene species 123. Intramolecular 58 amination led to intermediate 124, which finally evolved into indole 125 (Scheme 29).

Scheme 29 Iron-catalyzed synthesis of indole 125 from azirine 122.

<!-- image -->

Readily available azides proved to be convenient precursors of nitrenes as well. Accordingly, in a series of elegant studies, Driver developed complementary routes to indoles, which made use of b-styrylazides or o-vinylarylazides as substrates. 59 These rhodium-catalyzed processes could be performed under mild reaction conditions, 60 avoiding undesired by-product formation. For instance, 2-indole carboxylate 127 was obtained almost quantitatively from vinylazide 126 (Scheme 30, a), while the preparation of 2-aryl substituted indole 129 was accomplished using aryl azide 128 (Scheme 30, b). The extrusion of N2 followed by the formation of metal-nitrene intermediates 130 and 131 enabled regioselective intramolecular aminations to occur.

Scheme 30 Rhodium-catalyzed indole synthesis from azides.

<!-- image -->

Alternatively, Wang devised an indole synthesis from benzamides and EDA (ethyl diazoacetate, 133), which proceeded through a formal carbene insertion. 61 This transformation was based on the electrophilic activation of the amide with Tf2O in the presence of pyridine derivatives, while the diazo compound acted as nucleophile. In this manner, indole 137 was obtained in moderate yield by treatment of amide 132 with 133 and Tf2O, in the presence of pyridine derivatives (Scheme 31). The utility of this procedure was limited to the availability of diazo compounds; however, the functional group tolerance was remarkable. For instance, the presence of a labile C-I-bond, which could be problematic when using cross-coupling methodologies, was tolerated. The mechanism of this domino reaction would start with the formation of pyridinium adduct 134. A nucleophilic attack of the diazo compound could lead to intermediate 135. Then, an electrophilic aromatic substitution could afford species 136. Finally, an aromatization would lead to indole 137.

Scheme 31 Indole synthesis from benzamide 132 and EDA (133).

<!-- image -->

## Indole syntheses via processes involving a C-C- or N-N-cleavage

Valuable intermediates for indole synthesis such as o-alkynylanilines can be accessed from other sources than o-haloanilines, such as benzamides or ketoximes. These protocols are based on domino reactions which involve the cleavage of a CC-bond. For instance, Yanada reported a concise synthesis of N-carboxyl indoles from 2-alkynylbenzamides. 62 Hence, PhI(OAc)2 promoted a Hofmann-type rearrangement of benzamide 138 to isocyanate 139. A 5endo-dig platinum-catalyzed cyclization and a final esterification, efficiently afforded indole 140 (Scheme 32). With the use of appropriate bis-amides, this reaction was further applied to the synthesis of macrocyclic bis-indoles.

Scheme 32 Indole synthesis via Hofmann-rearrangement.

<!-- image -->

A multicatalytic one-pot reaction using 1-(2-alkynylphenyl) ketoxime derivatives to prepare N-acyl indoles was disclosed by Wu. 63 This protocol consisted of an indium and cyanuric chloride (CNC) co-catalyzed Beckmann-type rearrangement of a ketoxime such as 141, which afforded non-isolated o-alkynyl aniline intermediate 142. Subsequently, palladium-catalyzed cyclization in the presence of an excess of CuCl2 led to 3-chlorosubstituted indole 143 (Scheme 33).

Scheme 33 Indole synthesis via Beckmann-rearrangement.

<!-- image -->

A multicatalytic one-pot reaction using 1-(2-alkynylphenyl) ketoxime derivatives to prepare N-acyl indoles was disclosed by Wu. 63 This protocol consisted of an indium and cyanuric chloride (CNC) co-catalyzed Beckmann-type rearrangement of a ketoxime such as 141, which afforded non-isolated o-alkynyl aniline intermediate 142. Subsequently, palladium-catalyzed cyclization in the presence of an excess of CuCl2 led to 3-chlorosubstituted indole 143 (Scheme 33).

Scheme 33 Indole synthesis via Beckmann-rearrangement.

<!-- image -->

The use of N-acylbenzotriazole derivatives and internal alkynes was exploited by Nakamura to prepare 1,2,3-trisubstituted indoles. 64 In this interesting approach to indoles, the key step was the generation of a 2-aminobenzenediazonium species such as 146, a reminiscent intermediate of the Larock indole synthesis (see Scheme 11). Thus, cleavage of the N-N-bond of benzotriazole 144 at high temperature afforded intermediate 146 and set the stage for oxidative addition to form the arylpalladium species 147, which further inserted into the alkyne 145, yielding indole 148 (Scheme 34). 65

Scheme 34 Indole synthesis from benzotriazoles via N-N-bond cleavage.

<!-- image -->

## Indole syntheses through C-H-bond functionalizations

Modern transition metal-catalyzed transformations for a fast assembly of the indole core require the use of ortho-substituted anilines or 1,2-dihaloarenes, which should be prefunctionalized, thereby lengthening synthetic sequences and restricting to some extent their availabilities. Other attractive alternatives comprise the use of stoichiometric amounts of organometallic reagents, or relatively dangerous starting materials such as azides, diazo compounds or diazonium salts. An approach starting from mono-functionalized arenes, which should involve a C-H-bond functionalization, would be highly desirable. In this way, a wider collection of starting materials would be accessible and synthetic routes would be shortened, presenting this strategy as an attractive alternative to prepare indoles in a more efficient manner. This section summarizes recent achievements in indole syntheses via C-H-bond functionalization. 66

An intramolecular oxidative cyclization of N-aryl enamines was reported by Glorius. 67 This flexible procedure enabled the synthesis of, among others, functionalized indole 150 (Scheme 35). This palladium-catalyzed transformation made use of easy-to-prepare enamine 149 as starting material, however a large excess of a copper salt was required as terminal oxidant. Here, a large variety of anilines could be used since the transformation did not occur via electrophilic aromatic substitution, as was revealed by mechanistic studies, which supported a s-bond metathesis or a deprotonation for the creation of the new C-C-bond.

Scheme 35 Palladium-catalyzed oxidative cyclization of enamine 149.

<!-- image -->

Interestingly, enamines could be generated in situ via condensation and further transformed into the corresponding indoles in comparable yields. Later, several variants of this protocol were reported. Thus, this oxidative cyclization could be accomplished using copper or iron catalysts 68 or was promoted by iodine(III) compounds. 69

Downloaded by Brown University on 05 December 2012

On the contrary, a C-N-bond formation via palladium-catalyzed intramolecular amination of an aromatic C-H-bond was the strategy employed by Hartwig to prepare indoles. 70 This attractive approach made use of b-aryl oxime ester derivatives, such as 151, which was converted into indole 155 using a Pd(0) catalyst (Scheme 36). The neutral-redox conditions of this methodology should be noteworthy, since the use of external oxidants was suppressed. The proposed catalytic cycle likely started with a N-O-bond oxidative addition to oxime 151 to afford palladium species 152, which after a tautomerization set the stage for the C-H-bond cleavage. Palladacycle intermediate 154 would lead to the final indole through a C-N-reductive elimination.

Scheme 36 Palladium-catalyzed amination with oxime ester 151.

<!-- image -->

In terms of synthetic flexibility and operational simplicity, intermolecular oxidative couplings would be more convenient approaches to the indole core, as was cleverly devised by Fagnou. 71 Hence, in an evocation of the Larock indole synthesis, Fagnou developed a rhodium-catalyzed cyclization of protected anilines with alkynes. Extensive optimization studies allowed for the development of mild reaction conditions, which turned out in a remarkably ample scope and made possible the use of simple O2 as terminal oxidant. 72 For example, using anilide 156 and alkyne 157, 5-chloro-substituted indole 160 was obtained in good yield and with complete regioselectivity (Scheme 37). This remarkable transformation was proposed to occur through a sequence started with a C-H-bond cleavage. Rhodacycle intermediate 158 would undergo a carbo-rhodation into the alkyne to generate a new rhodacycle species 159, which evolved via C-N-reductive elimination to produce the desired indole 160. 73

Scheme 37 Intermolecular rhodium-catalyzed oxidative cyclization.

The most recent and remarkable discoveries in the syntheses of indoles are presented in this review.

## Summary and outlook

Syntheses of indole derivatives represent an amazing area of research for organic chemists. In addition, due to the relevance of indoles, efforts to develop their efficient syntheses influence other research areas, including biology, medicine and material science, thereby affecting our society and our life. The most recent and remarkable discoveries in the syntheses of indoles are presented in this review.

A plethora of versatile methodologies based on transition metal-catalyzed cyclizations, C-C- or C-N-bond formation crosscoupling reactions are currently available and allow for the assembly of functionalized indoles. While these protocols ensure the regioselective preparation of the desired indole derivative, they required a prefunctionalization of starting materials, which results in longer synthetic routes. In this sense, procedures involving functionalization of ubiquitous C-H-bonds have emerged as promising alternative routes to prepare indoles. However, the functionalization of strong C-H- or C-X-bonds involve, among others, the use of harsher reaction conditions or rather expensive catalysts, which proceed with low turnovers. Besides, synthetically elaborated substrates enable an easy access to specific indolecontaining structures through transition metal-catalyzed cyclizations, particularly, when using noble metals such as palladium, gold or platinum. In contrast, inexpensive copper or iron catalysts still remain a challenge to perform these valuable transformations.

As shown in this review, many cleverly devised routes for the synthesis of indoles have been developed in recent years. This could give the impression to the reader that not many new goals remain unravelled in this field. However, new solutions necessarily provoke new questions, which should be addressed. Thus, for example, sustainability and efficiency criteria appear as mandatory requirements. These translate into the necessity to put into practice protocols which make use of ecologically benign media, in terms of energy and cleanliness, starting from readily available feedstocks. Likewise, cascade-domino reactions or multi-step one-pot procedures which enable the straightforward preparation of highly functionalized indoles are highly desirable. Moreover, the development of robust catalysts might be useful in terms of functional group tolerance or catalytic efficiency, which should be beneficial in the preparation of complex indolecontaining structures or to accomplish large scale synthesis. As a consequence, new exciting and innovative achievements are expected to appear in this relevant research area.
